category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Numerical Methods — Iterative Methods Jacobi Gauss-Seidel

questiontext.text: The Jacobi iteration for \(Ax=b\) converges if the matrix \(A\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Strictly diagonally dominant

Sufficient condition for convergence of Jacobi: the matrix \(A\) is strictly diagonally dominant, i.e., \(|a_{ii}|>\sum_{j\neq i}|a_{ij}|\) for all \(i\). Under this condition, the spectral radius of the Jacobi iteration matrix \(\rho(D^{-1}(L+U))<1\), guaranteeing convergence. It also ensures Gauss-Seidel converges.

questiontext.text: Newton–Raphson method \(x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\) has order of convergence:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Quadratic (order 2)

Near a simple root \(r\) (where \(f'(r)\neq0\)): \(|e_{n+1}|\approx\frac{|f''(r)|}{2|f'(r)|}|e_n|^2\). The error squares at each step — quadratic convergence. For a root of multiplicity \(m>1\): Newton–Raphson has only linear convergence. The modified Newton \(x_{n+1}=x_n-m\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\) restores quadratic convergence.

questiontext.text: The Gauss-Seidel method differs from Jacobi in that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) It uses updated values immediately within each iteration

Gauss-Seidel: when computing \(x_i^{(k+1)}\), it uses already-updated values \(x_1^{(k+1)},\ldots,x_{i-1}^{(k+1)}\) (not the old values as in Jacobi). This typically leads to faster convergence — Gauss-Seidel usually converges about twice as fast as Jacobi. Both converge for strictly diagonally dominant or symmetric positive definite matrices.

answer.text: It uses already-updated values immediately in each iteration
It requires the matrix to be symmetric
It only works for upper triangular systems
It has slower convergence than Jacobi always

questiontext.text: An iterative method \(x^{(k+1)}=Mx^{(k)}+c\) converges for any starting vector if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The spectral radius \(\rho(M)<1\)

Convergence criterion: the iteration converges iff \(\rho(M)=\max|\lambda_i(M)|<1\). The error satisfies \(e^{(k)}\to0\) iff \(\rho(M)<1\). The rate of convergence is governed by \(\rho(M)\): smaller spectral radius = faster convergence. For the Jacobi matrix \(M_J=-D^{-1}(L+U)\).

questiontext.text: The condition number \(\kappa(A)=\|A\|\cdot\|A^{-1}\|\) for a symmetric positive definite matrix equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}\)

For a symmetric positive definite matrix: \(\|A\|_2=\lambda_{\max}\) and \(\|A^{-1}\|_2=1/\lambda_{\min}\), so \(\kappa_2(A)=\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}\). A large condition number indicates the system is ill-conditioned: small changes in \(b\) can cause large changes in the solution \(x\). This governs the accuracy of iterative methods.

questiontext.text: The bisection method for finding a root of \(f\) on \([a,b]\) (with \(f(a)f(b)<0\)) has order of convergence:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Linear (order 1), halving the interval each step

Bisection: \(|e_{n+1}|=|e_n|/2\). This is linear convergence with rate \(1/2\). After \(n\) iterations, the error is at most \((b-a)/2^n\). While reliable (always converges), it is slow compared to Newton-Raphson (quadratic) or secant method (superlinear). Number of iterations needed for tolerance \(\epsilon\): \(n\geq\lceil\log_2((b-a)/\epsilon)\rceil\).

questiontext.text: The LU decomposition writes \(A=LU\) where \(L\) is lower triangular and \(U\) is upper triangular. The system \(Ax=b\) is solved by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Forward substitution for \(Ly=b\), then backward substitution for \(Ux=y\)

LU solve: (1) \(A=LU\), (2) solve \(Ly=b\) (forward substitution, \(O(n^2)\)), (3) solve \(Ux=y\) (back substitution, \(O(n^2)\)). Total: \(O(n^2)\) per right-hand side after the \(O(n^3)\) factorization. Efficient for multiple right-hand sides.

answer.text: Back substitution for \(Lb=y\), then forward substitution for \(Uy=x\)
Forward substitution for \(Ly=b\), then back substitution for \(Ux=y\)

questiontext.text: The fixed-point iteration \(x_{n+1}=g(x_n)\) converges to the fixed point \(x^*=g(x^*)\) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|g'(x^*)|<1\)

Convergence criterion for fixed-point iteration near \(x^*\): if \(|g'(x^*)|<1\), the iteration is locally convergent (linear convergence with rate \(|g'(x^*)|\)). If \(|g'(x^*)|>1\), it diverges. If \(g'(x^*)=0\) (superlinear). Banach's contraction: global convergence if \(|g'(x)|\leq k<1\) for all \(x\) in the interval.

questiontext.text: The trapezoidal rule \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\approx\frac{h}{2}[f(a)+2f(a+h)+\cdots+2f(b-h)+f(b)]\) has truncation error of order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(O(h^2)\)

Trapezoidal rule: global error \(E=-\frac{(b-a)h^2}{12}f''(\xi)\) for some \(\xi\in(a,b)\), i.e., \(O(h^2)\). Simpson's rule has error \(O(h^4)\). Midpoint rule: error \(O(h^2)\). The trapezoidal rule is exact for polynomials of degree \(\leq1\) (linear functions); Simpson's rule is exact for degree \(\leq3\).

questiontext.text: For the \(3\times3\) system: \(4x+y+z=6\), \(x+5y+z=7\), \(x+y+4z=6\), Jacobi iteration:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Converges, since the system is strictly diagonally dominant

Row 1: \(|4|>|1|+|1|=2\). Row 2: \(|5|>|1|+|1|=2\). Row 3: \(|4|>|1|+|1|=2\). Strictly diagonally dominant \(\Rightarrow\) Jacobi converges. Unique solution: adding all equations gives \(6x+7y+6z=19\)... Actually solving directly: \(x=y=z=1\) satisfies all three (\(4+1+1=6,1+5+1=7,1+1+4=6\)).

answer.text: Convergence depends on the starting vector
Cannot converge since the matrix is not symmetric

questiontext.text: The classical 4th order Runge-Kutta method for \(y'=f(x,y)\) has local truncation error of order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(O(h^5)\) (local), \(O(h^4)\) (global)

RK4: local truncation error \(O(h^5)\); global error \(O(h^4)\). It uses 4 function evaluations per step: \(k_1=f(x_n,y_n)\), \(k_2=f(x_n+h/2,y_n+hk_1/2)\), \(k_3=f(x_n+h/2,y_n+hk_2/2)\), \(k_4=f(x_n+h,y_n+hk_3)\). Then \(y_{n+1}=y_n+h(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)/6\).

questiontext.text: The Lagrange interpolating polynomial through \(n+1\) points \((x_0,y_0),\ldots,(x_n,y_n)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(x)=\sum_{k=0}^n y_k\prod_{j\neq k}\frac{x-x_j}{x_k-x_j}\)

Lagrange form: \(P(x)=\sum_{k=0}^n y_k L_k(x)\) where \(L_k(x)=\prod_{j\neq k}(x-x_j)/(x_k-x_j)\). Each \(L_k(x_j)=\delta_{kj}\). The polynomial has degree \(\leq n\). Error: \(f(x)-P(x)=\frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}\prod_{k=0}^n(x-x_k)\).

questiontext.text: Simpson's rule \(\int_a^b f\,dx\approx\frac{h}{3}[f(a)+4f(m)+f(b)]\) (where \(m=(a+b)/2\)) is exact for polynomials of degree up to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 3

Simpson's rule uses a parabola through 3 points and is derived from the integral of the Lagrange interpolant. It is exact for polynomials of degree \(\leq3\) (even though it uses only 3 points — this is a superconvergence phenomenon). Error: \(-\frac{h^5}{90}f^{(4)}(\xi)\), so global error \(O(h^4)\).

questiontext.text: Starting with \(x_0=2\), one iteration of Newton–Raphson for \(f(x)=x^2-3\) gives \(x_1=\):
Gaussian elimination (without partial pivoting) for an \(n\times n\) system requires approximately how many arithmetic operations?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(O(n^3/3)\) multiplications/divisions

Gaussian elimination: forward elimination requires \(\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(n-k)^2\approx n^3/3\) multiplications. Back substitution: \(\sim n^2/2\). Total: \(O(n^3/3)\) dominant term. For large \(n\), this is approximately \(2n^3/3\) flops (floating point operations: each multiplication + addition counts as 1 flop in some conventions).

questiontext.text: Floating-point arithmetic introduces rounding errors. The machine epsilon \(\epsilon_m\) is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The smallest \(\epsilon\) such that \(fl(1+\epsilon)>1\)

Machine epsilon \(\epsilon_m\): the gap between 1 and the next floating-point number. For double precision (64-bit IEEE 754): \(\epsilon_m\approx2.22\times10^{-16}\). Any number \(x\) has relative rounding error \(\leq\epsilon_m/2\): \(|fl(x)-x|/|x|\leq\epsilon_m/2\). This bounds the accuracy of any single floating-point operation.

answer.text: The smallest \(\epsilon\) such that \(fl(1+\epsilon)>1\) in the floating-point system
The maximum absolute error in any computation

questiontext.text: The secant method has order of convergence approximately:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 1.618 (golden ratio, \(\phi=(1+\sqrt5)/2\))

Secant method: \(x_{n+1}=x_n-f(x_n)\frac{x_n-x_{n-1}}{f(x_n)-f(x_{n-1})}\). Order of convergence: \(\phi=(1+\sqrt5)/2\approx1.618\) (superlinear but subquadratic). Uses 1 function evaluation per step (vs 2 for Newton), so per function evaluation, it's comparable to Newton. More precisely: \(|e_{n+1}|\approx C|e_n|^\phi\).

questiontext.text: Partial pivoting in Gaussian elimination means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) At each step, swapping rows to bring the largest absolute value in the column to the pivot position

Partial pivoting: at each elimination step for column \(k\), find the row \(i\geq k\) with the largest \(|a_{ik}|\) and swap rows \(k\) and \(i\). This avoids division by small numbers (which amplifies errors). Partial pivoting ensures the growth factor is bounded and the computation is stable for most practical matrices.

answer.text: Permuting all rows and columns to maximize the pivot
Swapping rows to bring the largest absolute value in the current column to the pivot position
Using only the diagonal entries as pivots
Permuting the unknowns to improve sparsity

questiontext.text: For the 3×3 system parameterized by \(m\): \(\begin{pmatrix}m&1&1\\1&m&1\\1&1&m\end{pmatrix}x=b\), Jacobi iteration converges if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(|m|>2\)

The Jacobi matrix for this system has rows: \(x_1^{(k+1)}=(b_1-x_2^{(k)}-x_3^{(k)})/m\), etc. The iteration matrix is \(J=-\frac{1}{m}\begin{pmatrix}0&1&1\\1&0&1\\1&1&0\end{pmatrix}\). Eigenvalues of \(B=\begin{pmatrix}0&1&1\\1&0&1\\1&1&0\end{pmatrix}\) are \(2,-1,-1\). So eigenvalues of \(J\) are \(-2/m,1/m,1/m\). Spectral radius \(=2/|m|\). Convergence: \(2/|m|<1\Rightarrow|m|>2\). Strict diagonal dominance: \(|m|>|1|+|1|=2\). Same condition.

questiontext.text: The Gauss-Seidel iteration for \(2x+y=3\), \(x+3y=5\) starting from \((0,0)\): after the first complete iteration, \((x_1,y_1)=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \((3/2, 7/6)\)

Gauss-Seidel step 1: \(x_1=(3-y_0)/2=(3-0)/2=3/2\). Then use updated \(x_1\): \(y_1=(5-x_1)/3=(5-3/2)/3=(7/2)/3=7/6\). So \((x_1,y_1)=(3/2,7/6)\). True solution: \(x=2/5,y=7/5\) (solving: \(2x+y=3,x+3y=5\Rightarrow y=(5-x)/3\Rightarrow2x+(5-x)/3=3\Rightarrow6x+5-x=9\Rightarrow x=4/5,y=7/5\)). Wait: \(6x+5-x=9\Rightarrow5x=4\Rightarrow x=4/5\). Then \(y=(5-4/5)/3=21/15=7/5\).
